statusLine: EU fuel security remains under pressure with 16 of 27 countries holding critical reserves in at least one fuel type, despite overall stock levels above mandatory minimums.
fullAnalysis:
While EU-wide averages remain above the 90-day minimum (petrol 113 days, diesel 102.2 days, jet fuel 96.4 days), the distribution reveals significant vulnerabilities across member states. Romania faces the most severe shortages with all three fuel types in critical status, holding just 40.4 days of petrol and 30.2 days of diesel reserves. Poland and Luxembourg also show critical levels across all fuel categories.

Jet fuel emerges as the most constrained fuel type, with 16 of 27 countries holding critical reserves and an EU average of just 96.4 days. This reflects both the aviation sector's recovery and supply chain pressures following Middle East disruptions. Major economies including Germany, France, and Italy all report critical jet fuel stocks, while countries like Portugal (26.7 days) and Poland (25.3 days) show particularly concerning levels.

The 45.8% surge in Brent crude prices since late February ($72.48 to $105.64 per barrel) has translated into elevated pump prices across the EU, with petrol averaging €1.824/L and diesel €1.863/L. The Netherlands faces the highest petrol costs at €2.388/L, while Malta enjoys the lowest at €1.34/L, highlighting significant regional price disparities that may compound supply security concerns.
keyPoints: 16 of 27 EU countries report critical fuel reserves in at least one category, with Romania, Poland, and Luxembourg showing critical levels across all three fuel types
Jet fuel stocks are most constrained at 96.4 days EU average, with major economies Germany, France, and Italy all reporting critical levels
Brent crude has surged 45.8% since late February Middle East escalation, driving EU petrol prices to €1.824/L average
Eastern European countries show particular vulnerability, with Romania holding just 30.2 days of diesel reserves compared to the 90-day minimum
Price disparities reach €1.05/L between Netherlands (€2.388) and Malta (€1.34) for petrol, potentially exacerbating supply distribution challenges
